figures.gloss: The single supreme god who shaped the world from an egg of his own thought, setting the first seed of creation spinning within it; having made all things he withdraws above the firmament he raised.
figures.facet: A creator who makes by inward vibration rather than by hand or word — the cosmos as Amma's egg trembling into form, a maker known through his geometry more than through any cult.
figures.provenance: The cosmogony is well attested in Dogon ritual and Griaule–Dieterlen ethnography; the figure of Amma is secure, distinct from the contested astronomical glosses layered onto it.

figures.gloss: The cosmic egg (aduno kine, 'the world-egg') in which Amma planted the first seed; from its spiralling, vibrating germination all the categories of creation — the 266 signs, the eight grains, the directions — unfold.
figures.facet: Creation as a seed swelling toward a perfect helical figure: the Dogon render the whole made world as a single image-bearing egg, so that cosmology and geometry are the same act of reading.
figures.provenance: Central to Dogon cosmological symbolism; the egg-and-spiral imagery is well documented, while specific star-correlations remain academically contested.

figures.gloss: The first beings Amma made: amphibious, twinned, water-dwelling ancestors of perfect androgynous order. One Nommo is sacrificed and dismembered to purify the disordered cosmos, then resurrected and multiplied to bring water, speech and life to the earth.
figures.facet: A redeemer who is also the twins and the sacrifice in one body: the slain-and-risen saviour is plural and watery, his dismembered parts scattered to seed the world's directions before he rises whole.
figures.provenance: The Nommo sacrifice-and-resurrection is a core, well-attested Dogon mythologem; the 'amphibious instructors from Sirius' reading is the contested Griaule-derived layer and is not relied on here.

figures.gloss: The impatient firstborn who broke from his egg before completion, rebelling against Amma's order; his premature, incestuous descent introduced disorder, dryness and death, and he is changed into Yurugu, the Pale Fox who wanders the bush.
figures.facet: The fall told as prematurity, not pride: disorder enters the world because one twin could not wait to be finished — and the very fox who ruined creation becomes the Dogon's truest diviner, his pawprints in the sand read as oracle.
figures.provenance: Ogo/Yurugu and the fox-divination (yurugu/sand-table) are well attested; the precise astronomical exegesis attached to his egg is the contested material.

figures.gloss: The eight primordial Nommo ancestors, four male and four female pairs, from whom the lineages of humankind descend; they carry the eight seed-grains and the original speech down to the earth in the celestial granary.
figures.facet: Humanity descended not from one first man but from a counted set of eight paired ancestors — the human family rendered as an ordered, gendered octave rather than a single Adam.
figures.provenance: The eight ancestors and eight grains are firmly attested structural features of Dogon cosmology.

figures.gloss: The world-system descended from heaven by the Nommo: a stepped pyramidal granary whose ordered compartments hold the eight grains and map every creature and direction — the cosmos as an architecture lowered to earth on a thread.
figures.facet: The axis of the world imagined as a storehouse: order is a granary's grid, and to build a house, a field or a village is to re-inscribe the whole cosmic plan in miniature.
figures.provenance: The granary-descent and its geometric exegesis are central to Dogon ritual architecture, documented in Griaule and in standing village structure.

figures.gloss: The first ancestor to die, swallowed and then revived by a Nommo in serpent form; his death sanctifies the earth and binds the living to the soil, and the Hogon priest tends the altar where his life is renewed.
figures.facet: Death enters as gift, not curse: Lébé's dying makes the ground holy and arable, so that mortality itself is the seed by which the people are rooted to the land they farm.
figures.provenance: The Lébé cult and the Hogon priesthood are well-attested living institutions of Dogon religion.

figures.gloss: The great masked festival held once every sixty years, commemorating the first death and the descent of speech; its procession passes from village to village across years, renewing the covenant between the living, the ancestors and the made world.
figures.facet: A calendar measured in human lifetimes: the Sigui's sixty-year beat means most who dance it do so only once, the rite itself binding mortality to a cosmic cycle no single life can see whole.
figures.provenance: The sixty-year Sigui cycle is a well-documented Dogon institution; the claim that its period tracks the orbit of Sirius B is the contested astronomical interpretation, flagged as speculative.

figures.gloss: The senior priest and ritual head of a Dogon region or village group, guardian of the Lébé cult's altar and the highest uncontested religious authority in Dogon society. Hedged with strict taboos — he may not be touched, must be fed apart, and is bound to secluded conduct — he embodies the living pact between the community and the sanctified earth.
figures.facet: His authority rests entirely on restriction rather than display: the taboos that isolate him from ordinary contact are themselves the proof that he, and not any other office, carries the community's covenant with the soil.
figures.provenance: Marcel Griaule and Germaine Dieterlen, ethnographic fieldwork among the Dogon (1930s–1950s); the Hogon and Lébé-altar institution is uncontested, distinct from the disputed Sirius/Nommo astronomical material layered onto Dogon cosmology by later readings.

figures.gloss: The totemic clan cult of Dogon religion: each clan venerates an immortal ancestor-spirit, embodied in a totem animal, at a painted binou sanctuary tended by its own binou priest (binou kedine). The cult binds a lineage's living members to a specific animal, ancestor, and shrine.
figures.facet: It runs a second, parallel axis to the Lébé/Hogon system — where Lébé's cult binds the whole community to the soil in general, the Binou binds each clan separately to its own totemic ancestor and shrine.
figures.provenance: Griaule and Dieterlen fieldwork; the Binou cult is one of the well-documented, uncontested structural pillars of Dogon religion alongside the Lébé cult, ancestor veneration, and the mask society.

figures.gloss: The initiatory men's society that carves, owns, and dances the Dogon masks — kanaga, sirige, and dozens of others — and conducts the funerary rites that carry the dead out of the village and toward the ancestors. Its members alone know the sigi so, the secret ritual language used in its ceremonies.
figures.facet: The famous masks belong to a specific institution, not to Dogon religion in general: it is the Awa, not the Hogon or the Binou priests, that owns the funerary religion and speaks its secret language.
figures.provenance: Griaule and Dieterlen fieldwork; the Awa society, its masks, and the sigi so secret language are firmly attested, living institutions.

figures.gloss: The collective masquerade that closes the period of mourning: performed by the Awa some years after a death (often for several people at once), it lifts the deceased's soul out of the village and sends it on to join the ancestors, ending the restrictions the funeral imposed on the household.
figures.facet: It is the ordinary engine of Dogon funerary religion — far more frequent than the once-per-sixty-years Sigui — doing, household by household, the same work of transition the great festival does for the whole people at once.
figures.provenance: Griaule and Dieterlen fieldwork; the Dama is a well-documented, still-performed Dogon rite (and, since the 1930s, a documented draw for outside visitors to Dogon country).

edges.note: Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An aquatic, non-human entity that acts as the primal vessel or messenger for the creator's intelligence.
GOLD SEAM: Primal, enclosed spaces of immense kinetic vibration and heat where the structural blueprints of the universe are formed.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A highly structured ritual assembly that collapses time, integrating the community while directly recreating primordial divine events.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A specific, monumental architectural structure that contains both the physical remnants and the structural essence of the ancestors.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The supreme, remote creator who shapes the initial universe through signs and vibration before withdrawing.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Deep structural parallel in West African cosmologies regarding the androgynous, water-based twin who restores cosmic order.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The ambitious, disruptive twin who descends prematurely to dominate the earth, permanently introducing chaos.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The localized earth/animal figure whose intimate connection with the soil and eventual burial yields the secret of agriculture.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both use a mathematically precise ancestral matrix to map out the entirety of a people's spiritual and social reality.
The world issuing from a cosmic egg that already contains its ordering pairs — the Bön bipartite egg and Amma's egg of the world as the same world-egg figure, separate families, no contact.
GOLD SEAM: Supreme, transcendent African creators who originated the cosmos but withdrew from direct daily interference in human affairs.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both involve constructing localized architecture that perfectly maps to, and attempts to draw down, the cosmic order.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A loose structural parallel where a complex cosmic intention is compressed into a microscopic, abstract seed-form that gestates in darkness before manifesting.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Beings who physically walk the earth performing precise, structural work to reorganize and repair a flawed cosmic order.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A loose parallel—a highly specific, physicalized epicenter from which the diverse elements of creation burst forth.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Extremely complex, male-dominated secret society rites that involve intricate masks to commemorate the dead and renew the cosmos.
GOLD SEAM: Both traditions trace the flawed, difficult nature of the present world to a premature, rebellious act that fractured the creator's original design.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The literal embodiment of original ancestors within the vital physical tools of a culture's daily survival.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A disruption in the primordial order that irrevocably severs the perfect conduit between the earth and the sky, permanently establishing human hardship.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An agricultural storage and processing vessel elevated to a profound cosmic diagram, harboring the seeds of both literal and spiritual rebirth.
Within the tradition, Amma and the egg he thinks into being are one act of creation — the maker and the world-seed he sets spinning, inseparable.
Amma's first and perfect creatures are the Nommo; the supreme maker recedes and the watery twins become the working redeemers of his creation.
Ogo the impatient twin and the perfected, sacrificed Nommo are the two outcomes of Amma's paired creation — disorder broken loose against the order restored by sacrifice.
The eight ancestors are themselves Nommo offspring who carry the grains and speech to earth — the twin redeemer multiplied into the human lineages.
The eight ancestors descend in the celestial granary bearing the eight grains; ancestors and architecture come down to earth as a single ordered system.
Lébé, the first to die, is swallowed and revived by a Nommo in serpent form — the dying-rising redeemer extended into the founding ancestor of the soil.
The Sigui commemorates the first death and the gift of speech that Lébé and the ancestors brought — the rite that re-enacts the founding mortality.
The dismembered-and-resurrected redeemer whose scattered body fertilises the world — a dying-rising saviour rebuilt to renew creation, across unrelated families.
A primordial being sacrificed and parcelled out so that the ordered cosmos and its categories arise from his body — primordial-sacrifice as the engine of creation.
Both render the first divine reality as androgynous and paired before differentiation; a reach across families, flagged — twinned primordial wholeness.
Divine twins who pass through death to set the world right — but the Dogon twins are watery, androgynous redeemers where the Maya twins are hunters who outwit death.
A perfect being voluntarily sacrificed and resurrected to purify and redeem a disordered creation; a structural rhyme, flagged, with no contact implied.
Neighbouring West-African supreme makers who create and then withdraw from the world's affairs — the deus otiosus shared across the region, no proven common descent.
A supreme sky-source that recedes into impersonal remoteness above the working powers — the withdrawn high-god across unrelated families.
The single ground from which all categories of being unfold by inward vibration rather than external act; a reach toward Brahman as the source, flagged.
Both place an undifferentiated primal whole at the origin from which the ordered world emerges; world-egg beside the unseparated sky-earth, offered cautiously.
The infinite undifferentiated source contracting and unfolding into the categories of creation — world-egg as a rhyme with Ein Sof's emanation, flagged.
The disorder-bringing trickster who is nonetheless indispensable to divination and the working of fate — but Ogo's mischief is a fall, where Esu's is a necessary gate.
The kin-trickster whose impatience and rebellion introduce death and disorder into a made world — the adversary-from-within across families.
A primordial figure who seizes from the creator before the appointed time and so alters the human lot; a reach, flagged — premature theft against ordered patience.
Both pair a disordering trickster-power against an ordering creator-sibling; offered cautiously, the adversary as the necessary counterweight to order.
The first ancestor whose death sanctifies the earth and binds the people to the soil — mortality made the root of agriculture and lineage.
Lébé's altar is tended by the Hogon, the priest-king of Amma's order — the first death folded into the standing cult of the supreme maker.
Both seed humankind from a counted, ordered origin laid out in directions and grains/rivers — but the Dogon count eight paired ancestors against a single first couple; a flagged structural rhyme.
The world ordered along a single descending axis lowered from heaven — granary-architecture beside world-tree as the frame that holds the cosmos.
A stepped, tiered cosmic structure that maps every direction and being onto an axis; a reach across families, flagged.
Both lay the cosmos out as a quartered, direction-bearing plan radiating from a central order; offered cautiously as a cosmogram-rhyme.
A recurring ritual that re-enacts the founding passage through death to renew the covenant of the world; a distant structural parallel, flagged.
Both encode the whole cosmos as a single geometric figure to be read — the world-egg's spiral beside the quartered board; a flagged cosmogram-rhyme, no contact.
GOLD SEAM: Sacred architectures that function simultaneously as physical storage spaces for agriculture and profound cosmological maps.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent an absolute, somewhat distant creator principle in African cosmology whose architecture must be revealed through deep initiation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the structural necessity of a spontaneous deviation or 'error' to break a static perfection into active cosmic creation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A massive, cyclical communal festival that commemorates ancestral history and resets the social-spiritual clock.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A primordial cosmic being whose rigid, arrogant actions disrupt the universe's divine unfolding, requiring ultimate rectification.
GOLD SEAM: Architectural structures that serve as rigorous, mathematically precise microcosms of the entire universe.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A remote creator who sets the cosmos in motion but leaves the structuring of the earth to subordinate beings.
GOLD SEAM: Primordial, pluralized collectives that establish societal order through strict, indivisible mathematical unity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are primordial, quasi-divine teachers who imparted the original structuring wisdom to early humanity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Primordial, water-dwelling lords who hold the power of rain and forge foundational pacts or intermarriages with early humanity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the chaotic, primordial, and untamed stage of creation before order, agriculture, and law are established.
GOLD SEAM: Both are fiery, primordial beings who interrupt the creator's intended cosmic order through a premature act of rebellious pride.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are supreme cosmic architects who, after instigating creation, withdrew entirely from the immediate, daily spiritual administration of the world.
GOLD SEAM: Creation beginning not with a sudden expansion, but with an internal vibration and conceptual contraction within the divine.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A mythic ancestral visitor whose cyclical return is required to renew the agricultural and spiritual vitality of the people.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The 'failed' or demoted primal being whose disruption forms the necessary foundation for human culture.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A specific, foundational numerology of primal offspring that structures the entire subsequent society.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A multi-decade ritual cycle designed to execute a total generational transfer of cosmic and social authority.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The mundane labor and physical structures of daily agricultural/craft life infused with the exact map of cosmic salvation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The power of the spoken, vernacular word acting as a divine force that gives literal shape to the cosmos and society.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Creation emerges not from a spoken word, but from a profound internal bodily pressure or gestation within the primordial creator.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A foundational mythic figure whose civilizing actions are inextricably linked to a primal incestuous act that profoundly disrupts cosmic order.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both embody the necessary, chaotic breaking of cosmic order and static rules, which serves as the catalyst for individuation and dynamic change.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are chthonic figures deeply tied to the earth and death who serve as the primary source of profound renewal and guidance for the living.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A singular, elevated Creator God associated with the sky, weather, and the ultimate ordering of reality.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A primordial figure whose disruptive, world-altering actions irrevocably separate the perfect divine realm from the flawed mortal earth.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A monumental, cyclical ritual strictly governing the transition of generations and the redistribution of social power.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The shadow-companion to the creator who prematurely acts on his own, thereby introducing chaos, mortality, and death into the grand design.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Highly exclusive, masked male secret societies whose elaborate rituals physically enact the terrifying presence of the ancestors to maintain order.
GOLD SEAM: Massive, cyclic choreographies that do not merely depict myth, but are believed to structurally renew the world's life force through precise movement.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A loose structural parallel in utilizing sequential, deeply symbolic initiatory processes to transmit deep cultural memory and spiritual authority across generations.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Aquatic, serpentine progenitor figures who bring life and order to humanity from the water.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are massive, socially binding ceremonies focused on world renewal, purification, and the cyclical resetting of communal time.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are highly intelligent but spiritually rebellious actors who isolate themselves to improperly engineer creation, bringing chaos to the original harmony.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both systems completely anthropomorphize cosmic reality, mapping the fundamental shape and potential of the universe directly onto the idealized human anatomy.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Primordial, semi-divine ancestors who survive a cosmic transition to seed and populate the earthly realm.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are physical structures deliberately built to serve as a microcosm of the universe, mapping the cosmic order onto a local ritual space.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A loose structural parallel: architectural constructs (a sledge vs. a granary) that physically house the cosmological order.
GOLD SEAM: Both involve advanced, non-human entities originating from specific star systems (often Sirius) who descend to impart civilizational leaps.
GOLD SEAM: The primordial cosmic egg containing the radiant blueprint of all creation before it bursts into materialized duality.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are structured, physical microcosms containing all the raw materials and cosmic seeds necessary to represent the universe.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A massive, highly synchronized inter-village ritual cycle that temporarily resets social debts and passes generational authority.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The localized earth-guardian whose physical anchoring in the settlement ensures the community's stability and protection.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The concentrated, infinitesimal geometric seed that contains the structural blueprint for all dimensions of the universe.
GOLD SEAM: A rhythmic, communal ritual meant to re-align the community with cosmic justice and ancestral truth.
GOLD SEAM: Elaborate, highly structured cyclical rites that require an excruciating journey to align the human microcosm with a renewed celestial macrocosm.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A specific, physical architectural space that serves as the uncorrupted structural blueprint for cosmic order.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The primordial, smooth, undifferentiated shape containing the supreme potential from which all diverse creation explodes.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An overarching, self-contained supreme creator who orders the cosmos but relies on intermediate forces for daily governance.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A massive, socially binding ceremonial engine that collapses time, bridging the gap between the living and the ancestral continuum.
GOLD SEAM: The singular, supreme creator deity who is completely abstract, yet from whose formless will all the manifold universe vibrated into existence.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Dogon cosmology and Swedenborgianism both envision the fundamental structure of the universe as taking the form of an idealized, colossal human body.

Quadruplet/twinned culture-bearing ancestors at the origin of the lived world and its waters — the four Caracaracol brothers from whom the sea pours and the Dogon Nommo pairs of the primal waters; a loose parallel of the multiple first-ancestors, flagged as a reach.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both act as cosmic disruptors who break the static, pre-ordained order of the universe, introducing the necessary chaos that allows for individuation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The singular, nurturing cosmic parent whose generative power creates and sustains all life without acting as a wrathful judge.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both act as the unmanifest cosmic seed containing the potential structure for all material creation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both systems place supreme cosmic weight on the veneration and literal preservation of the primordial ancestors as the duty of the living.
GOLD SEAM: Both are structural, architectural models intended to facilitate and receive the descent of cosmic, extraterrestrial ancestors.
GOLD SEAM: Both describe extraterrestrial creators who descended to Earth in spacecraft to instruct primitive humanity in civilization, biology, and science.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A highly complex, mathematically structured generational cycle governing the transfer of spiritual and social responsibilities across time.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The supreme creator pre-existing in a sealed enclosure (the pearl/egg) before expanding outward to generate the cosmos.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A highly structured ritual assembly that literally collapses time, allowing participants to recreate and participate in the primordial divine council.
GOLD SEAM: The initial, unmanifested shape that holds the divine blueprint of the cosmos before breaking open to form material reality.
Intra-tradition: the Hogon is the living priest who tends the altar where Lébé's renewed life is kept — the office and the founding ancestor's cult are one working system.
Intra-tradition: the Hogon's taboo-hedged person makes visible, at village scale, the covenant with the sanctified order Amma set in motion and then withdrew from.
Intra-tradition: each clan's Binou ancestor-spirit descends from the same ancestral genealogy the Eight Ancestors seed, the totemic cult being the clan-level expression of the larger lineage system.
Intra-tradition: the Awa carves and dances the masks used at the Sigui as well as in ordinary funerary rites — it is the standing institution the sixty-year festival draws on.
Intra-tradition: the Awa is precisely the mask society that owns and performs the Dama, the ordinary end-of-mourning masquerade.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: male initiatory mask societies that keep a secret ritual language, own the funerary rites, and enforce social order through masked performance.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: a hedged, taboo-restricted sacral office whose person's isolation from ordinary contact is itself the proof that he carries the community's covenant with a founding sacred order.

dossier.essence: The traditional religion of the Dogon, a farming people of the Bandiagara escarpment in east-central Mali. It centers on a withdrawn supreme creator, Amma, who makes the world from a cosmic egg or seed; primordial amphibious ancestor-spirits, the Nommo; and the disorder loosed by the rebellious 'pale fox,' Ogo/Yurugu. It is renowned — and academically contested — for the elaborate symbolic cosmology recorded by Marcel Griaule.
dossier.cosmos: Amma conceives the world within an egg whose vibrating, spiralling germination unfolds into the ordered categories of creation — grains, directions, and signs. The first creatures, the androgynous water-dwelling Nommo, embody perfect order; one is sacrificed, dismembered, and resurrected to purify and water the world, while the impatient twin Ogo breaks from his egg prematurely, bringing dryness, disorder, and death, and becomes the Pale Fox. Humanity descends from eight Nommo ancestors who bring the eight seed-grains and speech to earth in a celestial granary, and death enters as a gift through the first ancestor, Lébé.
dossier.practice: Religion is organized around the Lébé earth-cult tended by the Hogon priest; masked funerary and renewal rites, above all the great Sigui festival held roughly every sixty years and processing village to village; and fox divination, in which questions traced in sand overnight are read from the tracks the pale fox leaves. Cosmology is materialized in village plan, granary architecture, and the geometry of ordinary building.
dossier.canon: An oral and ritual corpus with no scripture; the principal published sources are Griaule's 'Conversations with Ogotemmêli' (1948) and Griaule and Germaine Dieterlen's 'Le Renard pâle' (1965).
dossier.reflects: Cosmos as text — the rendering of the whole made world as a single readable geometric figure (egg, spiral, granary, signs), so that to build a house or trace the fox's tracks is to read the plan of creation.
sources: Marcel Griaule & Germaine Dieterlen, 'Le Renard pâle' (1965)
Walter E.A. van Beek, 'Dogon Restudied: A Field Evaluation of the Work of Marcel Griaule' (1991)
